h1Title: Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is a squamous-cell cancer arising from the epithelium of the nasopharynx, the upper part of the throat behind the nose. Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) drives the majority of non-keratinizing cases worldwide, accounting for over 133,000 new diagnoses annually with the highest incidence in southern China, Southeast Asia, and North Africa. The most common presentation is a painless neck lump from metastatic cervical lymph nodes, often accompanied by unilateral nasal obstruction and blood-stained nasal discharge. NPC is highly radiosensitive; concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiotherapy achieves 5-year overall survival rates above 80% for early-stage disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 133,354 new cases and 80,008 deaths worldwide in 2020 (GLOBOCAN 2020)
keyStats.demographics: Incidence 20-30 per 100,000 in endemic southern China versus under 1 per 100,000 in Western Europe and North America; male-to-female ratio roughly 2.5:1
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence age 40-60 in endemic regions; a smaller peak in adolescents and young adults is characteristic
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 133,000 new cases per year (GLOBOCAN 2020); roughly 70% occur in East and Southeast Asia

definition: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (ICD-10: C11) is a malignancy originating from the mucosal epithelium lining the nasopharynx, a 4 cm × 4 cm cuboidal space above the soft palate and behind the nasal cavity. The World Health Organization classifies NPC into three histological types: Type 1 (keratinizing squamous-cell carcinoma), Type 2 (non-keratinizing differentiated carcinoma), and Type 3 (non-keratinizing undifferentiated or poorly differentiated carcinoma). Type 3 accounts for about 95% of cases in endemic regions and is the form most strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection. The nasopharynx is not accessible to routine endoscopy or examination; tumors grow silently until they invade the skull base, obstruct the Eustachian tube, or metastasize to cervical lymph nodes — explaining why most patients present at Stage III or IV. Staging follows the AJCC/UICC 8th edition, which uses MRI-based T, N, and M classification. NPC is managed jointly by head and neck oncologists, radiation oncologists, and medical oncologists. Despite anatomical inaccessibility that makes surgery impractical, NPC's exceptional radiosensitivity means that intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) with concurrent cisplatin is curative in the majority of locoregionally advanced cases.

typesClassification.description: Accounts for 5-25% of cases in non-endemic populations; less strongly EBV-associated; behaves like conventional head-and-neck squamous-cell carcinoma with lower radiosensitivity and worse prognosis than Type 3.
Intermediate form with partial differentiation; EBV-associated in endemic settings; less common than Types 1 or 3.

Dominant form in endemic regions (up to 95%); densely infiltrated with reactive lymphocytes; consistently EBV-positive; highly radiosensitive with 5-year survival above 70-80% when treated with modern chemoradiotherapy.

primarySymptoms: Painless unilateral or bilateral neck mass from metastatic cervical lymphadenopathy, occurring in 70-80% of patients at first presentation and often the sole symptom for weeks to months.
Unilateral nasal obstruction with a sensation of fullness or blockage, caused by tumor growth into the posterior nasal cavity.
Epistaxis or blood-tinged nasal discharge (posterior nasal bleed), typically intermittent and often ignored as 'simple nosebleed'.
Unilateral conductive hearing loss or persistent ear fullness from Eustachian tube obstruction by tumor, frequently misdiagnosed as otitis media with effusion in adults.
Tinnitus, usually unilateral, coinciding with Eustachian tube dysfunction and middle-ear effusion.
Diplopia (double vision) from involvement of cranial nerve VI (abducens) as the tumor erodes the skull base, indicating T3 or T4 disease.
Headache, often unilateral and persistent, from tumor invasion of the skull base or dural involvement.
Facial pain or numbness from trigeminal nerve involvement as disease extends along the foramen ovale or foramen lacerum.
Trismus (limited jaw opening) from invasion of the pterygoid muscles, signaling advanced local extension.
Hoarseness or dysphagia from lower cranial nerve involvement (IX, X, XII) with jugular foramen invasion.
earlyWarningSigns: New unilateral serous otitis media in an adult with no prior ear history — Eustachian tube obstruction by an NPC is a classic presentation that warrants nasopharyngoscopy.
Recurrent unilateral posterior epistaxis in a young adult or adolescent from an endemic region without obvious septal pathology.
Persistent painless upper cervical lymph node larger than 1 cm for more than 4 weeks in the absence of infection.
Elevated EBV viral capsid antigen (VCA-IgA) or early antigen (EA-IgA) titers on serological screening in an endemic-region adult.
emergencySigns: Sudden cranial nerve palsy (diplopia, facial numbness, hoarseness) with a known or suspected neck mass — intracranial extension warrants urgent MRI.
Severe unilateral epistaxis that does not respond to anterior nasal packing, requiring endoscopic evaluation to exclude vascular tumor erosion.
Progressive dysphagia with aspiration risk from lower cranial nerve involvement in advanced NPC.
New-onset seizure, confusion, or severe headache in a patient under treatment for NPC — intracranial disease progression or leptomeningeal involvement must be excluded.

causes.description: EBV DNA is detectable in virtually all WHO Type 3 NPC specimens; viral proteins LMP1 and LMP2A drive oncogenesis by inhibiting apoptosis and activating NF-κB signaling. Elevated EBV VCA-IgA and EA-IgA antibody titers precede clinical NPC by years and are used for screening in high-risk populations.

Specific HLA-A, HLA-B, and HLA-C alleles common in Cantonese and Fujianese Han Chinese are associated with 3-5 times higher NPC risk versus other ethnic groups. Familial clustering occurs; first-degree relatives of NPC patients carry 4-10 times baseline risk.

causes.cause: Dietary and environmental carcinogens — salted fish and nitrosamines
causes.description: Regular consumption of Cantonese-style salted fish early in life (particularly in childhood) is associated with 2-3 times increased NPC risk. Salted fish contains volatile nitrosamines that are genotoxic and may act as co-carcinogens with EBV.

Smoking is associated with an approximately 2-fold increased NPC risk, predominantly for Type 1 (keratinizing) carcinoma in non-endemic regions. Smoking may impair EBV immune surveillance and promote carcinogenesis in already-infected epithelial cells.

Occupational exposure to formaldehyde (woodworkers, embalmers, textile workers) and to cotton textile dust has been linked to modestly elevated NPC risk in case-control studies in China and North Africa.

causes.cause: Previous radiation to the head and neck
causes.description: Prior therapeutic irradiation of the nasopharynx or adjacent structures carries a small but real risk of secondary NPC or second-primary squamous carcinoma, typically developing 10-20 years after exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Southern Chinese or Southeast Asian ethnicity
riskFactors.description: Incidence in Guangdong province of China reaches 20-50 per 100,000; Cantonese migrants to North America retain 5-10 times the local host-country risk, confirming a genetic component over and above environmental factors.
NPC is the second most common cancer in adolescents and young adults in Algeria, Morocco, and Tunisia, with distinct EBV strain differences from Asian disease.
Men are diagnosed approximately 2.5 times more often than women globally, possibly related to higher smoking rates and occupational exposures.

Cantonese salted fish consumption before age 10 confers 2-3 times increased adult NPC risk in case-control studies (PMID 7729839). Dietary transition away from preserved foods partly explains falling endemic incidence.
Smoking roughly doubles NPC risk particularly for keratinizing histology; cessation reduces risk over 10+ years.
First-degree relatives of NPC patients carry 4-10 times baseline risk. Sibling pairs in Guangdong show concordance rates consistent with shared HLA and EBV susceptibility alleles.

Positive EBV VCA-IgA titers carry an approximately 20-fold relative risk for subsequent NPC development. Population screening in Guangdong detects NPC at stage I-II in over 70% of seropositive screened individuals.

affectedDemographics: Endemic belt: southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi, Hunan), Hong Kong, Taiwan, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Vietnam, and the Philippines carry the highest incidence globally.
North Africa (Algeria, Tunisia, Morocco): second highest global incidence; Maghrebi young adults and adolescents are disproportionately affected.
Men outnumber women 2.5:1 across all populations; median age at diagnosis 45-55 in endemic regions versus 50-65 in non-endemic settings.
Migrants from endemic regions to the UK, USA, Canada, and Australia retain elevated but declining incidence over generations, supporting partial environmental causation.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a thorough head and neck history and examination, followed by nasopharyngoscopy — the single most important step. Flexible or rigid nasopharyngoscopy visualizes the primary tumor in the lateral pharyngeal recess (fossa of Rosenmüller) in 80-90% of cases; biopsy of any suspicious lesion is performed under local anaesthesia at the same sitting. If nasopharyngoscopy is inconclusive but clinical suspicion is high, a guided biopsy under general anaesthesia is arranged. EBV serology (VCA-IgA, EA-IgA) is positive in over 90% of Type 3 NPC and supports the diagnosis in ambiguous cases. Plasma EBV DNA quantification by quantitative PCR is the most sensitive blood-based marker (sensitivity 96%, specificity 93% in HKSTAR 2 trial) and is used for staging, treatment response monitoring, and detection of recurrence. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the head and neck is the staging investigation of choice, mapping skull-base involvement, perineural extension, and retropharyngeal lymph nodes more accurately than CT. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis or whole-body PET-CT is performed for distant metastasis staging. Fine-needle aspiration of accessible cervical nodes confirms metastatic squamous-cell carcinoma or undifferentiated carcinoma when the primary is not visible. Staging follows AJCC/UICC 8th edition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualization and tissue sampling of the primary tumor in the nasopharynx
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera is passed through the nose under local anaesthetic spray; the nasopharynx is examined and biopsies taken. The procedure takes 10-15 minutes; mild discomfort, no overnight stay required.

diagnosticTests.test: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of head and neck
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines primary tumor size, skull-base erosion, perineural spread, and retropharyngeal lymph node involvement for T and N staging
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes in the scanner. Intravenous contrast is injected. The patient must remain still. Results reviewed by a specialist radiologist within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive blood-based marker for NPC; used for diagnosis support, staging prognostication, treatment response, and post-treatment surveillance
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Levels fall rapidly with successful treatment; persistent or rising values after therapy indicate residual or recurrent disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening and diagnostic support, particularly in endemic regions; positive in 90%+ of non-keratinizing NPC
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; results in 3-7 days. Positive titers are not diagnostic alone but increase pre-test probability substantially.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects distant metastases to lung, bone, liver, and other sites; upstages approximately 10% of MRI-staged locoregional NPC to stage IVc
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Injection of a radioactive glucose tracer, rest for 60 minutes, then 20-30 minutes in the scanner. Mild fatigue; mildly radioactive for 24 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Fine-needle aspiration cytology of cervical lymph node
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms malignant cells in cervical neck mass when nasopharyngoscopy is inconclusive
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Thin needle inserted into the neck node under ultrasound guidance; mild pressure, takes 5-10 minutes. Result within 24-48 hours in most centres.

treatmentOverview: The NCCN 2024 and ESMO 2021 guidelines recommend concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CCRT) as the standard of care for stages II-IVb NPC, with the addition of neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy in locoregionally advanced cases. Intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) delivers 70 Gy in 33-35 fractions to the primary tumor and involved nodes while sparing the parotid glands, spinal cord, and brainstem. Concurrent weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m² or three-weekly cisplatin 100 mg/m² is given during radiation over 6-7 weeks. For stage III-IVb disease, the current standard is neoadjuvant chemotherapy — three cycles of gemcitabine plus cisplatin (GP), or docetaxel–cisplatin–fluorouracil (TPF) — followed by concurrent CCRT, based on the phase III SYSUCC-001 and GEM-FORCE trials demonstrating a 5-10% improvement in failure-free survival versus CCRT alone. Stage I disease is treated with radiotherapy alone achieving 5-year survival above 90%. For distant metastatic (stage IVc) or recurrent disease, platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (cisplatin plus gemcitabine) is first-line with an objective response rate of 60-75%. Anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (camrelizumab, tislelizumab, pembrolizumab) improves progression-free survival in recurrent/metastatic NPC; camrelizumab is approved in China. Surgical salvage is considered for small local recurrences after radiation, using endoscopic or robotic-assisted nasopharyngectomy; neck dissection treats persistent nodal disease confirmed on PET-CT or rising EBV DNA at 10-12 weeks. Plasma EBV DNA undetectable at 8 weeks post-treatment is the primary surrogate marker of treatment success and guides decisions on adjuvant chemotherapy. Supportive care throughout treatment — mucositis management, hearing protection, nutritional support, and physiotherapy — substantially influences quality of life and treatment completion rates.

medicalTreatments.name: Intensity-modulated radiotherapy (IMRT) — 70 Gy in 33-35 fractions
medicalTreatments.description: The primary curative modality. IMRT delivers high-dose radiation to the nasopharynx and involved lymph nodes while minimizing dose to adjacent salivary glands, spinal cord, and brainstem. Treatment runs over 6-7 weeks, Monday to Friday.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year local-control rates 85-95% for T1-T2 disease; 75-85% for T3-T4 with concurrent cisplatin (PMID 22583583)

medicalTreatments.name: Concurrent cisplatin (100 mg/m² q3wk or 40 mg/m² weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Radiosensitizes NPC cells and eliminates micrometastatic disease; given during the 6-7 weeks of radiotherapy. Three-weekly dosing has more documented evidence but weekly dosing is used when renal function or tolerance is a concern.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Addition of concurrent cisplatin improves 5-year OS by approximately 10% versus radiotherapy alone (PMID 16079782)

medicalTreatments.name: Neoadjuvant gemcitabine-cisplatin (3 cycles before CCRT)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard induction regimen for stage III-IVb NPC per ESMO 2021 and NCCN 2024. Gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m² days 1 and 8, cisplatin 80 mg/m² day 1, every 21 days for 3 cycles, then CCRT.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 3-year failure-free survival improved from 76% to 85% versus CCRT alone in the GEM-FORCE trial (PMID 32598480)

medicalTreatments.name: Cisplatin-gemcitabine doublet (recurrent/metastatic)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line chemotherapy for recurrent or metastatic NPC; gemcitabine 1,000 mg/m² days 1 and 8 plus cisplatin 80 mg/m² day 1 every 21 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rate 60-75%; median progression-free survival 7-8 months in registration trial (PMID 26869311)

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-PD-1 immunotherapy (camrelizumab, tislelizumab, nivolumab)
medicalTreatments.description: PD-L1 expression and EBV neoantigens make NPC particularly immunogenic. Pembrolizumab and other anti-PD-1 agents show durable responses in pretreated disease; camrelizumab is approved in China for recurrent/metastatic NPC.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective response rate 25-35% as monotherapy; combinations with chemotherapy improve ORR to 75-91% in early trials

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic or robot-assisted salvage nasopharyngectomy
surgicalOptions.description: For small, accessible local recurrences (rT1-rT2) after definitive radiotherapy. Robotic-assisted approach via transoral or transnasopharyngeal route removes recurrent tumor with negative margins.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year OS after salvage nasopharyngectomy 40-60% for rT1-rT2; complications include palatal fistula, Eustachian tube dysfunction, and rare internal carotid artery injury

surgicalOptions.name: Neck dissection for persistent or recurrent cervical lymphadenopathy
surgicalOptions.description: Selective or modified radical neck dissection is performed when PET-CT or plasma EBV DNA indicates persistent nodal disease 10-12 weeks after CCRT.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Regional control rates of 75-90% after salvage neck dissection in appropriate candidates

alternativeTreatments.name: Plasma EBV DNA-guided surveillance
alternativeTreatments.description: Post-treatment plasma EBV DNA monitoring every 3 months detects molecular recurrence before clinical or radiological relapse by a median of 6 months, enabling earlier salvage treatment. HKSTAR 2 demonstrated survival benefit from EBV DNA-guided intensification.

whySeeSpecialist: NPC diagnosis and management require a multidisciplinary team including an ENT head-and-neck surgeon for nasopharyngoscopy and biopsy, a radiation oncologist with IMRT expertise, and a medical oncologist for systemic therapy. Skull-base involvement and cranial nerve palsies require neurosurgical input. Audiological monitoring is mandatory because of radiation-induced ototoxicity.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a tertiary head-and-neck cancer centre accredited for IMRT, with access to plasma EBV DNA testing and PET-CT. In endemic regions, population-based EBV DNA screening programs exist at selected hospitals in Guangdong, Hong Kong, and Singapore. International patients frequently travel to Hong Kong and Singapore for definitive treatment.
hospitalCriteria: IMRT radiotherapy unit with head-and-neck immobilization capability
Plasma EBV DNA quantitative PCR laboratory on-site or with rapid access
MDT (multidisciplinary team) with ENT, radiation oncology, and medical oncology
PET-CT for staging and treatment response assessment
keyFacilities: Queen Mary Hospital / Prince of Wales Hospital (Hong Kong) — high-volume NPC centres
Sun Yat-sen University Cancer Center, Guangzhou
Major cancer centres in Malaysia (Hospital Kuala Lumpur, Institut Kanser Negara)
Specialist head-and-neck oncology centres in any country for expatriate or migrant patients
preventionStrategies: Reduce consumption of preserved, salted fish and other nitrosamine-containing foods, particularly in childhood, in endemic populations.
Avoid tobacco smoking, which roughly doubles NPC risk especially for keratinizing histology.
Participate in EBV VCA-IgA or plasma EBV DNA screening programs if living in or originating from an endemic region and aged 30-60.
Limit occupational exposure to formaldehyde, wood dust, and other industrial upper-respiratory carcinogens with appropriate protective equipment.
Report persistent unilateral ear fullness, unexplained neck lumps, or recurrent posterior epistaxis promptly — early-stage NPC is curable.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all post-treatment plasma EBV DNA surveillance appointments (typically every 3 months for 3 years) to detect molecular recurrence early.
Perform daily mouth-opening exercises from the start of radiotherapy to prevent trismus, continuing for 6-12 months after treatment ends.
Maintain meticulous dental hygiene with fluoride gel trays throughout and after radiotherapy — osteoradionecrosis of the jaw is prevented primarily through oral care.
Use saliva substitutes, chewing gum, or pilocarpine lozenges for radiation-induced xerostomia to improve quality of life.
Annual audiological assessment is recommended after high-dose ear-region radiation; hearing aids are effective for radiation-induced sensorineural loss.
Abstain from tobacco and limit alcohol intake throughout treatment and recovery.

recommended: High-calorie, high-protein soft foods during chemoradiotherapy (smoothies, fortified yoghurt, soups) to maintain body weight and reduce treatment interruptions
Adequate hydration — at least 2 L daily — to support cisplatin renal clearance and prevent mucositis
Vitamin E and zinc-rich foods (nuts, seeds, legumes) to support mucosal healing
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) to reduce inflammation during treatment
avoid: Salted, pickled, and fermented preserved foods, particularly Cantonese salted fish, which contain nitrosamines linked to NPC causation
Spicy or acidic foods during mucositis to reduce oropharyngeal irritation
Alcohol during chemoradiotherapy — increases mucositis severity and hepatotoxicity risk
Tobacco in any form throughout treatment — impairs healing and may reduce radiosensitivity

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic activity (30 minutes walking, 5 days per week) is encouraged before and after treatment. During chemoradiotherapy, fatigue often limits intensity — gentle walking and daily physiotherapy exercises for the neck and jaw are realistic goals. Resistance training is encouraged 6 weeks after completing treatment to address cancer-related fatigue and sarcopenia.
dailyManagement: Rinse the mouth with saline or sodium bicarbonate solution after every meal to reduce mucositis during radiotherapy.
Use a nasal saline irrigation device twice daily to clear post-nasal secretions and prevent sinusitis during and after treatment.
Perform jaw-opening stretches (TheraBite or tongue depressor stacking) for 5 minutes three times daily to prevent trismus.
Apply fluoride gel (1.1% neutral sodium fluoride) in custom trays nightly to protect irradiated teeth from radiation caries.
Monitor for Eustachian tube dysfunction symptoms — grommets or tympanostomy tubes relieve chronic middle-ear effusion in approximately 70% of patients.
prognosis: Prognosis is closely linked to stage at diagnosis. Stage I NPC treated with radiotherapy alone achieves 5-year overall survival above 90%. Stage II-III disease treated with concurrent chemoradiotherapy achieves 5-year overall survival of 75-85%. Locoregionally advanced stage IVa-IVb disease treated with neoadjuvant chemotherapy plus CCRT achieves 5-year survival of 55-75%. Distant metastatic disease (stage IVc) carries a 5-year survival below 20% with conventional chemotherapy, though anti-PD-1 combinations improve durable responses in approximately 20-30%. Plasma EBV DNA undetectable at 8 weeks after treatment strongly predicts relapse-free survival; persistent EBV DNA is an independent predictor of relapse. Recurrence after IMRT is predominantly distant (45-55%) rather than local in modern series. Patients diagnosed through EBV DNA population screening in Guangdong at stage I achieve over 97% 5-year survival.
recoveryTimeline: Acute mucositis and dysphagia peak at weeks 3-5 of radiotherapy and resolve over 4-8 weeks after completion. Xerostomia, fatigue, and dysgeusia persist for 6-12 months. Plasma EBV DNA should be undetectable by week 8 post-treatment. Surveillance MRI and EBV DNA are performed every 3 months for 3 years, then 6-monthly to year 5.
complications: Radiation-induced xerostomia (dry mouth) from parotid gland damage occurs in 50-80% of patients despite IMRT parotid-sparing; pilocarpine and saliva substitutes improve quality of life.
Sensorineural hearing loss from cochlear irradiation; annually audiologic monitoring and early hearing-aid fitting reduce communication disability.
Trismus from pterygoid muscle fibrosis; daily jaw-opening exercises started during radiotherapy reduce severe trismus incidence from 30% to under 10%.
Hypothyroidism from neck irradiation affects 20-30% of patients; annual TSH monitoring with levothyroxine replacement is standard.
Osteoradionecrosis of the mandible or skull base — rare (<2%) but serious; prevented by pre-treatment dental extraction of unhealthy teeth and rigorous post-radiation oral hygiene.
Radiation-induced fibrosis of neck soft tissues with restricted head movement and carotid artery stenosis — long-term cardiovascular monitoring is recommended.

supportResources.name: NPC Society (Hong Kong)
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and information resource for NPC patients in Hong Kong and diaspora communities.

supportResources.name: NCCN Guidelines — Head and Neck Cancers
supportResources.description: Freely accessible NCCN clinical practice guidelines covering NPC staging and treatment algorithms.

supportResources.name: Cancer Research UK — Nasopharyngeal Cancer
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient information covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sensorineural hearing loss is a cochlear or auditory nerve disorder presenting with gradual bilateral high-frequency hearing loss; NPC causes predominantly conductive, unilateral hearing loss from middle-ear effusion, not cochlear damage. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy and MRI distinguish Eustachian-tube NPC obstruction from primary cochlear disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peritonsillar Abscess / Tonsillitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peritonsillar abscess and tonsillitis cause acute throat pain, fever, and tonsillar exudate. NPC causes painless nasal obstruction and neck lumps with minimal pharyngeal erythema. The primary tumor arises in the nasopharynx above the soft palate, not in the palatine tonsils, and is invisible without nasopharyngoscopy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cervical Lymph Node Tuberculosis (Scrofula)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cervical TB lymphadenopathy is often painless and can closely mimic NPC neck metastases. TB nodes may have central necrosis on CT and are acid-fast bacilli positive on FNAC; NPC nodes show malignant squamous cells on cytology and plasma EBV DNA is elevated. A chest X-ray, FNAC, and EBV DNA together distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deviated Nasal Septum
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Septal deviation causes bilateral or alternating nasal obstruction, not unilateral posterior blockage; it does not cause neck lumps or hearing loss. Anterior rhinoscopy shows the septal deflection; nasopharyngoscopy is clear. NPC causes asymmetric posterior obstruction with an abnormal nasopharyngeal mucosa.

relatedConditions.name: Tinnitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Eustachian tube dysfunction from NPC frequently causes unilateral tinnitus with middle-ear effusion; tinnitus may be the presenting symptom.

relatedConditions.name: Sensorineural Hearing Loss
relatedConditions.relevance: High-dose radiotherapy to the cochlea causes radiation-induced sensorineural hearing loss in 20-40% of long-term NPC survivors.

relatedConditions.name: Lung Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Lung is the most common site of distant metastasis from NPC; staging CT/PET must include thorax.

relatedConditions.name: Otosclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both otosclerosis and NPC cause progressive unilateral conductive hearing loss in adults and enter the differential diagnosis before nasopharyngoscopy.

faqs.question: What is nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
faqs.answer: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) is a cancer arising from the lining of the nasopharynx, the space behind the nose and above the throat. It is driven by Epstein-Barr virus in most endemic cases and is most common in southern China, Southeast Asia, and North Africa. About 133,000 new cases occur worldwide annually.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of nasopharyngeal carcinoma?
faqs.answer: The most common first sign is a painless lump in the neck from a cancerous lymph node. Other early signs include unilateral nasal blockage, blood-tinged nasal discharge, and persistent unilateral ear fullness or hearing loss. Because the nasopharynx is hidden, symptoms are often present for months before diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is nasopharyngeal carcinoma curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, NPC is highly curable when detected at stages I-II; 5-year survival exceeds 90% for stage I treated with radiotherapy alone. Even stages III-IVb achieve 55-85% 5-year survival with chemoradiotherapy. The key is early detection, which is why EBV DNA blood screening programs in endemic regions are important.

faqs.question: Does NPC spread to the neck?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cervical lymph-node metastasis occurs in 70-80% of NPC patients at diagnosis, making a painless neck lump the most common presentation. The jugulodigastric and upper deep cervical nodes are most commonly affected. This nodal disease responds well to radiotherapy.

faqs.question: Is Epstein-Barr virus the cause of NPC?
faqs.answer: EBV is present in virtually all non-keratinizing NPC tumors and is considered the primary oncogenic driver combined with genetic susceptibility and environmental factors. EBV viral capsid antigen antibodies are detectable years before tumor development and are used for population screening in endemic regions.

faqs.question: How is nasopharyngeal carcinoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires nasopharyngoscopy with biopsy of the primary tumor. Supporting tests include plasma EBV DNA PCR, EBV serology, and gadolinium MRI of the head and neck. PET-CT or CT chest-abdomen-pelvis is performed for distant staging. Fine-needle aspiration of neck nodes confirms malignant cells.

faqs.question: What is the main treatment for NPC?
faqs.answer: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy — intensity-modulated radiotherapy (70 Gy over 7 weeks) combined with cisplatin chemotherapy — is the standard for stages II-IVb. Neoadjuvant gemcitabine-cisplatin before chemoradiotherapy is added for stage III-IVb. Stage I is treated with radiotherapy alone. Surgery plays only a salvage role.

faqs.question: What are the side effects of NPC radiotherapy?
faqs.answer: Common side effects include mucositis, xerostomia (dry mouth), sensorineural hearing loss, dysphagia, trismus, fatigue, and hypothyroidism. Modern IMRT substantially reduces xerostomia by sparing the parotid glands. Most acute effects resolve within 3 months; hearing loss and dry mouth may be long-term.

faqs.question: What is plasma EBV DNA testing in NPC?
faqs.answer: Plasma EBV DNA is a blood test measuring circulating viral DNA released by NPC cells. It is positive in 95%+ of active NPC. It is used to confirm diagnosis, assess staging, monitor treatment response, detect recurrence, and screen high-risk populations. Undetectable EBV DNA after treatment strongly predicts good prognosis.

faqs.question: Can NPC come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Recurrence occurs in roughly 20-30% of patients, mostly as distant metastases. Local recurrence in the nasopharynx occurs in 5-10% with modern IMRT. Rising plasma EBV DNA during surveillance is an early marker of recurrence, usually detectable 6 months before clinical relapse.

faqs.question: Is NPC more common in certain ethnicities?
faqs.answer: Yes. Southern Chinese (Cantonese, Fujianese) and Southeast Asian populations have incidence rates 20-50 times higher than Europeans. North Africans (Algerian, Moroccan, Tunisian) have the second-highest incidence globally. Genetic HLA susceptibility alleles and EBV exposure patterns explain much of this disparity.

faqs.question: Does eating salted fish cause NPC?
faqs.answer: Regular consumption of Cantonese-style salted fish, particularly in childhood, is associated with a 2-3 times increased NPC risk. Salted fish contains volatile nitrosamines that act as co-carcinogens alongside EBV. Falling consumption of preserved foods partly explains decreasing NPC incidence in Hong Kong over recent decades.

faqs.question: Can NPC cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. Double vision (diplopia) from abducens (cranial nerve VI) palsy occurs when NPC erodes the skull base or cavernous sinus. It indicates at least T3 disease. Facial numbness from trigeminal nerve involvement may accompany it. Both symptoms should prompt urgent MRI and ENT referral.

faqs.question: How is NPC staged?
faqs.answer: NPC is staged using AJCC/UICC 8th edition based on MRI: T1-4 describes local tumor extent, N0-3 nodal involvement, and M0-1 distant metastasis. Stage I is T1N0M0; stage IVc is any T, any N, M1. MRI is superior to CT for T-staging; PET-CT detects M1 disease in about 10% of cases.

faqs.question: Is surgery used for NPC?
faqs.answer: Surgery is not first-line because the nasopharynx is anatomically inaccessible and NPC responds well to radiation. Endoscopic or robotic-assisted nasopharyngectomy is used for small local recurrences after radiotherapy, and neck dissection treats persistent or recurrent cervical lymphadenopathy.

simpleName: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC, throat cancer behind the nose)
metaDescription: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma: EBV-linked cancer behind the nose causing neck lumps and hearing loss. Staging, IMRT chemoradiation, and survival rates explained.

sources.title: Chen YP, Chan ATC, Le QT, Blanchard P, Sun Y, Ma J. 2019. Nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Lancet 394(10192):64-80.

Chan KCA, Woo JKS, King A, et al. 2017. Analysis of plasma Epstein-Barr virus DNA to screen for nasopharyngeal cancer. N Engl J Med 377(6):513-522.
National Comprehensive Cancer Network. NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Head and Neck Cancers. Version 2.2024.
